Title: Role of Anthocyanidin Reductase, Encoded by BANYULS in Plant Flavonoid Biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Variety: Arabidopsis Col-0; tobacco transgenic system not specified
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Condensed tannin (proanthocyanidin) branch downstream of ANS
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces anthocyanidins to the corresponding 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols using NADPH or NADH
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Animal and human health benefits; antioxidant activity; contributes to flavor and astringency; protects ruminants against pasture bloat and improves nitrogen nutrition
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Arabidopsis seed coat endothelium; transgenic tobacco petals; transgenic Arabidopsis leaves; Medicago seed coat
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes condensed tannin accumulation and reduces anthocyanin pigmentation when ectopically expressed
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant protein enzyme assay; HPLC; HPLC-MS; GC-MS; CD spectroscopy; transgenic overexpression in tobacco and Arabidopsis; DMACA staining; butanol-HCl CT quantification
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern: AtBAN is expressed in seed endothelium at onset of fertilization through preglobular embryo stage; MtBAN strongly expressed in young seeds, weakly in flowers/flower buds, very weakly in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species: Young developing seeds (MtBAN); seed endothelium during early seed development (AtBAN)
Pathway_Genes.Promoters: Cauliflower mosaic virus 35S promoter, constitutive (for ectopic expression)
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: branch-switch enzyme competing with anthocyanin formation; key control point supported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering condensed tannins into forage legumes such as alfalfa to create bloat-safe forage, improve protein utilization and silage quality, reduce greenhouse gas and urine-N losses; also for modifying health properties, flavor, and astringency of fruits, vegetables, tea, and wine
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Ectopic expression reduces anthocyanin pigmentation in flowers/leaves; possible color/flavor changes
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: cDNA library sequencing; no accession reported for omics dataset
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAN from Arabidopsis thaliana and Medicago truncatula encodes anthocyanidin reductase rather than the previously hypothesized leucoanthocyanidin reductase. 2. Recombinant BAN converts cyanidin, pelargonidin, and delphinidin into the corresponding 2,3-cis-flavan-3-ols, including (-)-epicatechin. 3. Ectopic BAN expression in tobacco petals and Arabidopsis leaves decreases anthocyanins and induces condensed tannin accumulation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper demonstrates no LAR activity for BAN with leucocyanidin/dihydroquercetin assays. Minor trans products likely arise from epimerization artifacts. Study species for metabolic engineering experiments include tobacco and Arabidopsis, but the focal genes are from Arabidopsis and Medicago truncatula.
